exemplar.title: Do the participants in Squid Game have freedom of choice and are the organizers morally responsible?

detailedExemplarData.title: Do the participants in Squid Game have freedom of choice and are the organizers morally responsible?

strengths: Explicit and precise identification of the philosophical issue in both title and introduction
Clear justification linking specific plot points (phone recruitment, voting, concealment) to themes of consent and coercion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student fully meets the topâband descriptors for Identification and Justification by explicitly naming the issue of free will and moral responsibility and by clearly connecting stimulus details to that issue. All strand assessments support the maximum score of 3.

strengths: Logical overall structure with recognizable sections (introduction, argument, counterâargument, conclusion)
Generally coherent paragraph transitions and clear topic sentences
weaknesses: Occasional abrupt breaks and incomplete paragraphs disrupt flow
Minor awkward phrasing and nonstandard terminology (e.g. âutilitarianisticâ)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Clarity sits comfortably in the band for âstructured and generally organizedâ work that can be âeasily followed.â Despite some interruptions in flow and phrasing issues, the organization and coherence justify a solid midâlevel score of 3 out of 4.

strengths: Accurate integration of key philosophical concepts (Sartreâs radical freedom, Rousseauâs social contract, utilitarianism)
Mostly correct use of relevant terminology throughout
weaknesses: Explanations occasionally simplified or superficial (e.g. contract and voting mechanisms)
Minor spelling and phrasing lapses reduce precision
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates mostly accurate, relevant knowledge with satisfactory explanations and generally appropriate vocabulary use, matching the description for a bandâ3 performance in Knowledge and Understanding.

strengths: Analytical engagement in weighing Sartre versus Rousseau and interrogating utilitarian justifications
Use of stimulus examples (recruitment call, voting system) to support arguments
weaknesses: Analysis often remains cursory and lacks deeper development
Limited breadth of examples (primarily narrative) and minimal real-world parallels
Counter-arguments identified but not fully analyzed or nuanced
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the investigation moves beyond mere description and uses examples and counterâpositions, the analysis lacks sustained development and depth, placing it in the lower half of the 3â4 band. A score of 3 out of 8 reflects this calibrated performance.

strengths: Engages with alternative viewpoints (freedom vs. coercion, utilitarianism)
Justifies most main claims with reference to informed consent and consequences
Clear conclusion consistent with preceding argument
weaknesses: Evaluation of alternative interpretations is uneven and somewhat oneâsided
Leaps in logic remain unaddressed; justification not provided for every point
Limited exploration of additional ethical perspectives
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation shows some balanced treatment of viewpoints and justification of key points, culminating in a clear conclusion, but it lacks the thoroughness and sophistication of higher bands. A midârange score of 3 out of 6 accurately reflects these strengths and limitations.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student accurately outlines Sartreâs radical freedom, Rousseauâs social contract critique of inequality and basic utilitarian calculus. Although explanations are brief and occasionally simplified, they remain mostly correct and relevant to the issue, satisfying the descriptor of âmostly accurate and relevant knowledge with satisfactory explanation.â

Alternative interpretationsâfreedom vs coercion, altruism vs exploitationâare evaluated with some balance, yet the treatment is uneven and occasionally one-sided. The student weighs utilitarian justification but quickly dismisses it, showing some evaluation but not the thoroughness required for higher bands.

The final paragraph reiterates the central claim and ties it coherently to earlier premises about informed consent, coercion and utilitarian failure. The conclusion follows logically from the preceding analysis and is clearly worded, fulfilling the descriptor for a âclear conclusion consistent with argument,â though not exceptionally sophisticated, warranting the lower end (5) of the top band.

The investigation follows a recognisable structure: introduction, exposition of affirmative case, counter-case, moral responsibility section and conclusion. While some subsection breaks are abrupt and pagination markers intrude, ideas generally appear in a logical sequence without serious digressions, corresponding to âstructured and generally organized.â

The student gives a detailed explanation of how the television series generates questions of coercion, informed consent and responsibility. Specific plot points (phone call recruitment, voting mechanism, concealment of lethal stakes) are directly linked to philosophical themes, demonstrating a clear and explicit justification of the connection between stimulus and issue.

The student acknowledges a pro-freedom stance (Sartre) and a coercion-based critique (Rousseau) and offers utilitarian defences and rebuttals. Analysis of these counter-positions is present but remains brief and somewhat assertive, consistent with identifying counter-arguments without fully convincing analysisâhence the 3-4 band.

The student explicitly states the philosophical issue in the title and restates it verbatim in the introduction, making the question of free will and moral responsibility unmistakable. The wording is precise (âDo the participants in Squid Game have freedom of choiceâ¦?â) and framed as a philosophical problem rather than merely a plot summary, fully matching the top-band descriptor for explicit identification.

Examples are drawn mainly from the stimulus (phone-number recruitment, majority vote, first-round massacre). These are appropriate and generally support the argument, but the investigation relies on a single narrative source and gives limited real-world parallels, aligning with the mid-band descriptor (3-4) rather than higher bands expecting stronger breadth.

Key philosophical termsââradical freedom,â âsocial contract,â âutilitarianism,â âmoral responsibilityââare used largely correctly and integrated into sentences. Mis-spelling (âutilitarianisticâ) and the odd vague phrase occur but do not dominate, matching the band for vocabulary used âmostly appropriately.â

Major claims are typically supported by at least a brief rationale (e.g., lack of informed consent undermines freedom; utilitarian defence fails due to excessive suffering). Several leaps in logic remain unaddressed, so justification is present for many but not âall or nearly allâ points, placing it in the 3-4 range.

The essay moves beyond description by weighing Sartre against Rousseau and by interrogating utilitarian justifications, showing genuine analytical engagement. Nevertheless, the reasoning is sometimes cursory and doesnât probe objections in depth, so it fits the 3-4 mark band: analysis present but lacking full development.

Readers can follow the line of thought without difficulty: each paragraph signals its purpose, transitions are adequate and the concluding section clearly summarises findings. Minor repetition and occasional awkward phrasing prevent it from reaching the âclear and coherent throughoutâ standard, but it comfortably meets the band in which the answer can be âeasily followed.â

annotations.comment: The research question is stated explicitly as a philosophical issue, clearly identifying the problem of freedom and responsibility.
Consider rephrasing the question into a declarative thesis to enhance academic tone and clarity.
Strong justification of how specific plot elements (phone recruitment, voting, concealment) link to philosophical themes of consent and coercion.
The introductory narrative is overly descriptive; consider condensing stimulus details to focus more quickly on the philosophical issue.
The paragraph on voluntary participation is well structured with clear topic sentences outlining phone sign-up, contract signing, and voting.
Explanation of the contract and voting mechanisms remains superficial; deepen the philosophical analysis of choice conditions.
Effective use of Sartreâs concept of radical freedom to support the claim that actions arise from genuine choice.
Expand the existential analysis by addressing Sartreâs notion of bad faith to nuance the discussion of coercion versus self-deception.
Insightful counter-analysis: the lack of informed consent effectively undermines claims of genuine freedom.
Well-chosen Rousseau reference demonstrates understanding of how social inequalities coerce choice.
Clarify how manipulation differs philosophically from coercion, perhaps by defining consent conditions.
Smooth transition into the moral responsibility section guides the reader effectively to the next issue.
Introduce a brief roadmap indicating the upcoming utilitarian discussion to improve coherence.
The paragraph is cut mid-sentence, which disrupts understanding; ensure complete paragraphs.
Consider integrating another perspective (e.g., Kantian duty ethics) to enrich the evaluation of organizer responsibility.
The euthanasia analogy is intriguing but under-examined; explore its ethical limits and counterarguments.
Strengthen authority by citing original Rousseau passages or secondary literature on the social contract.
This paragraph ends abruptly, interrupting the flow; ensure the full explanation is included.
Analysis effectively links social inequality to psychological coercion using external pressures on participants.
Distinguish between act and rule utilitarianism to clarify which form underpins the organizersâ defense.
Strong refutation that excessive suffering outweighs benefits aligns well with utilitarian calculus critique.
The term âutilitarianisticâ is nonstandard; use âutilitarianâ for accuracy and professional tone.
The conclusion succinctly reiterates the thesis and ties it effectively to preceding analysis.
Final paragraph confirms resolution of issues but could be enhanced by reflecting on real-world coercion beyond the fictional context.
The essay closes with a clear, decisive statement affirming that organizers bear moral responsibility.

Appropriate use of philosophical vocabulary throughout

Well-chosen examples that lend strong support
Conclusion stated but not entirely consistent
All or nearly all points justified
